questions.q: This sutta is also called "The Great Forty." What is the number forty referring to?
questions.a: Twenty wholesome qualities that support the noble eightfold path, and twenty unwholesome qualities that oppose it. For each of the eight factors, the Buddha names the supporting and opposing states and the work of cultivating or abandoning them. The path is not one thing — it is a web of forty mutually reinforcing (or undermining) qualities.

questions.q: The Buddha distinguishes two levels of right view (sammādiṭṭhi). What are they?
questions.a: Mundane right view (lokiya) — understanding kamma, that actions have consequences, that there is a next world, that contemplatives can awaken. And supramundane right view (lokuttara) — the direct wisdom that arises with the noble path, that sees the Four Noble Truths clearly in experience. Both are valid and necessary; the supramundane does not replace the mundane.

questions.q: The sutta makes a bold claim about the role of right view. What is it?
questions.a: Right view is the forerunner (pubbaṅgama). Without it, the other seven factors of the path cannot develop correctly. The Buddha compares it to the leader of a convoy: the whole group goes where the leader goes. Your right view determines what the other factors of your practice actually become.

questions.q: Each factor has three supporting qualities. What are the three for any given factor?
questions.a: Right view of that factor (you know what it is and why it matters), right effort toward that factor (you work to develop it), and right mindfulness of that factor (you watch its presence and absence). These three — view, effort, mindfulness — are the engine that develops every other factor of the path.

questions.q: The sutta lists specific wrong livelihoods the practitioner must abandon. Can you name at least two?
questions.a: Trickery, cajoling, hinting, belittling, pursuing gain with gain. In modern terms: any work whose structure depends on deceiving others or exploiting their weaknesses for your benefit. The sutta is not abstract; it is specific about how wrong livelihood actually looks in daily transactions.

questions.q: How does this sutta differ from "right view" as presented in Sāriputta's MN 9?
questions.a: MN 9 unfolds right view through the chain of dependent origination and the Four Noble Truths applied to phenomenon after phenomenon. MN 117 situates right view structurally within the eightfold path, showing how it leads the other seven and has both a mundane and a supramundane dimension. MN 9 is experiential depth; MN 117 is structural architecture. A full practice reads both.
